chapters.label: Mortgage and collateral families
chapters.script:
[DIYA] At nine in the morning, one hundred crore rupees sits in controlled accounts. By noon, that money will belong to different people in different amounts. Nothing about the cash itself reveals the answer. The documents and current transaction state do.

[MEERA] Borrowers created the money through mortgage interest, scheduled principal, prepayments, and recoveries. The servicer reconciled it. Now the collection report must separate each component before anyone starts paying claims.

[DIYA] Treating the entire hundred crore as one bucket would be the first error. Interest and principal can have different permitted uses. Recoveries may follow a defined classification. Reserve releases and hedge receipts may enter through separate doors.

[MEERA] The first claim is often not an investor. Servicing, trusteeship, administration, account banking, and other senior expenses keep the transaction operating. Their priority protects the machinery that delivered the cash.

[DIYA] Expense definitions still require scrutiny. An uncapped extraordinary cost can consume cash unexpectedly. A model that assumes last year's routine fee forever may fail precisely when replacement servicing or enforcement becomes expensive.

[MEERA] After expenses, reserve requirements are tested. The reserve was drawn last month because several borrower payments arrived late. Replenishing it today reduces current distributions but restores protection for the next interruption.

[DIYA] That choice reveals the waterfall's time dimension. Cash can pay an investor now or rebuild a buffer for later. Priority determines which objective wins when both cannot be satisfied.

[MEERA] Senior interest comes next under our assumed documents. The calculation uses the opening note balance, applicable rate, day count, and any prior shortfall. A label reading senior interest is not a number until those terms are applied.

[DIYA] Junior interest follows only after higher claims are satisfied. If available interest runs out, the unpaid amount may defer, accrue, trigger another consequence, or be lost under the specific terms. The word junior describes vulnerability, not a universal legal result.

[MEERA] Principal now enters. Under sequential payment, available principal retires the senior note before junior principal receives anything. The senior balance falls and subordination can become thicker relative to what remains.

[DIYA] Under pro rata payment, principal is shared among eligible classes. Junior capital returns sooner, but senior protection may build more slowly. Neither rule is inherently correct; each creates a different allocation of time and loss.

[MEERA] At ten-thirty, the delinquency report arrives. The ratio has crossed its trigger by a narrow margin. The waterfall state changes from pro rata to sequential for the current or next period according to the measurement convention.

[DIYA] One basis point can redirect crores because the trigger converts a measured condition into a contractual action. The size of the consequence comes from the clause, not from the size of the threshold breach.

[MEERA] Residual cash that would have returned to the originator is now trapped. It replenishes protection or accelerates senior principal. The originator loses current upside while investors gain additional defence.

[DIYA] This is sometimes described casually as cash being diverted. The precise description is better: the transaction stopped releasing value at the bottom of the priority and retained it for specified senior purposes.

[MEERA] Now imagine the same collateral inside a pass-through certificate. Net borrower principal would reach investors more directly, so prepayment would change investor timing immediately. The holder remains close to the underlying mortgage clock.

[DIYA] In a pay-through arrangement, the issuer can reshape collections through note schedules, reserves, classes, and triggers. The distance between borrower and investor grows, but every additional gate must be modelled and legally supported.

[MEERA] Distance can create stability. A liquidity reserve may keep note interest current while borrowers pay late. Distance can also create opacity if investors look only at a smooth expected schedule and forget the uneven collateral feeding it.

[DIYA] At eleven, a swap payment is due. The assets pay fixed rates while some notes pay floating rates. The hedge supplies cash under the current rate environment and ranks at a specified point in the waterfall.

[MEERA] If the swap counterparty fails, the expected receipt can disappear. A termination amount may become payable and have a different priority. Counterparty risk enters the same payment day even though no borrower changed behaviour.

[DIYA] The model now rolls every ledger forward. Opening collateral minus scheduled principal, prepayment, and default adjustments becomes closing collateral. Opening notes minus principal paid and write-downs become closing notes.

[MEERA] The reserve performs its own reconciliation. Opening cash plus replenishment and permitted income minus draws equals closing reserve. Counting a drawn reserve as though it still exists would double the protection with one spreadsheet error.

[DIYA] Interest shortfalls also survive into later periods if the documents say they accrue. Deleting them understates claims. Compounding them without authority overstates claims. The model must be literal where the legal prose is literal.

[MEERA] Losses enter through another route. A borrower default removes principal from the performing balance. Recovery may arrive months later. The waterfall or loss-allocation provisions decide which ledger or tranche absorbs the net shortfall first.

[DIYA] Payment order and loss order can point in opposite directions. Senior notes are paid first and lose last. Residual interests are paid last and generally feel deterioration first. That asymmetry is the economic foundation of tranching.

[MEERA] Suppose defaults rise but recoveries remain high. Timing can still hurt because collateral interest stops and recovery arrives late. A model that enters only ultimate net loss misses the liquidity and duration consequences before recovery.

[DIYA] Suppose prepayments accelerate instead. Principal cash rises today, senior notes may shrink faster, and future interest income falls. A performing borrower can change note value without creating a credit loss.

[MEERA] The Public Securities Association benchmark helps compare mortgage prepayment speeds. It is a convention applied to projections, not a statement that borrowers have agreed to follow a curve.

[DIYA] At eleven-thirty, the analyst notices the trigger denominator used last month's closing collateral rather than the balance specified for today's test. Correcting it moves the ratio below the threshold. The entire payment allocation changes again.

[MEERA] That is why model review is not cosmetic. A formula can redirect money just as surely as a contractual amendment. Inputs, dates, definitions, and trigger states require traceability to the source documents.

[DIYA] Public disclosure may show an illustrative waterfall. The executed documents govern. If a prospectus summary omits cure mechanics, principal deficiency, hedge termination, or enforcement priority, the model cannot fill those gaps by habit.

[MEERA] Reserve Bank of India directions establish requirements around securitisation transactions in India. They help define the regulatory boundary. They do not provide the missing numerical terms of our hypothetical payment day.

[DIYA] The right response to missing terms is a visible diligence gap. It is not a market-standard assumption quietly coloured blue. An unknown priority can reverse a tranche conclusion.

[MEERA] Now the hundred crore is almost exhausted. Senior expenses were paid. The reserve was restored. Senior interest was current. Junior interest partly deferred. Sequential principal reduced the senior note. No residual cash escaped.

[DIYA] Another transaction with the same hundred crore could produce a different outcome because its reserve target, trigger state, hedge, note balances, and priority differ. Collateral cash does not carry its destination inside it.

[MEERA] At noon, the trustee sends the distribution report. Investors see amounts, but the report should also explain balance movements, shortfalls, reserve changes, trigger results, and the bridge from collections to payments.

[DIYA] Transparency means an independent reader can reconstruct the allocation. Every unit of cash has one source, one permitted use, and one effect on future protection.

[MEERA] Now take the same reasoning to a vehicle-loan transaction. Principal arrives faster because borrowers sell cars. Sequential payment reduces senior notes. Used-car values later weaken, but the additional subordination built earlier changes who absorbs loss.

[DIYA] Move again to a revolving card pool. During the healthy revolving period, principal buys new receivables. After an early-amortisation trigger, principal pays investors. One stream changes purpose because the contractual state changed.

[MEERA] Move once more to distressed loans. Recoveries arrive irregularly after cost. The same priority governs each arrival, but years may pass between distributions. Timing becomes part of loss even before principal is formally written down.

[DIYA] Pass-through, pay-through, payment priority, and cash-flow modelling are therefore four layers of one problem. First decide how close investors remain to collateral timing. Then identify how notes reshape it. Then read who is paid. Finally execute those rules across changing periods.

[MEERA] Confusion disappears when the layers stay separate. A pass-through label cannot replace a waterfall. A waterfall diagram cannot replace a model. A model cannot invent rights missing from the documents.

[DIYA] The hundred crore was never merely a pile of money. It was borrower behaviour translated through servicing, accounts, legal priority, trigger state, and arithmetic.

[MEERA] A securitisation pays correctly only when the cash, the contract, and the model tell the same story down to the last rupee.
